text: Three years ago, he was found to have advanced ascending colon cancer infiltrating the liver in the S-six region. He underwent open-right hemicolectomy and partial resection of the S-six region. He was found to have liver metastasis, so he underwent laparoscopic partial liver lateral segmentectomy one year and three months after the initial surgery after another chemotherapy. Eleven months after the second surgery, he had a swollen paraurtic lymph node, and it was diagnosed as a metastasis of the colon cancer by PET-CT. He underwent laparoscopic paraurtic lymphadenectomy twenty-one months after the second surgery.
The surgeon performs laparoscopic dissection to separate severe adhesions between the abdominal wall and the omentum, ensuring safe access to the layer containing the target lesion.

The surgeon exposes the inferior vena cava and the left renal vein behind the duodenum and head of the pancreas. Dissection is performed around the left renal vein, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and associated nerve tissue. A metastatic lymph node, approximately two centimeters in size, is exposed, surrounded by the pancreatic parenchyma, polar vein, right crux of the diaphragm, and liver caudate lobe.
The surgeon clips the feeding artery of the metastatic lymph node and cuts the artery, ensuring minimal bleeding during the resection of the lymph node.
In the metastatic lymph node, only CK-VII was immunostained, but neither CDX-II nor CK-XX was immunostained. It was more probably differentiated than the primary cancer of the ascending colon.

He had no severe postoperative complications but had a little lymphuria and stayed at the hospital for eleven days after the surgery. He has had no recurrences for two years and four months after the laparoscopic parotid lymphadenectomy with no chemotherapy after the surgery. Even in patients with multiple previous abdominal surgeries, laparoscopic surgery may be useful if the target lesion is in a previously undetected layer. Importantly, patients with a history of multiple laparotomy may be increasing in the future due to improvements in the results of systemic therapy.
